segments.title: Four books for approaching medieval warfare
segments.summary: The medieval-themed reading list opens with Montgomery's illustrated survey of warfare and its maps of major campaigns.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke introduces four books that approach medieval conflict through military institutions, crusading, Mediterranean history, and Anglo-French rivalry. He begins with Bernard Montgomery's concise survey, recommending its battle maps and broad coverage despite the black-and-white presentation.

evidence.note: The video establishes its medieval theme and introduces Montgomery's illustrated history of warfare.

segments.title: Norman cavalry, fortifications, and crusading expansion
segments.summary: A reading on Norman warfare links heavy cavalry and strategic fortification to expansion in England, southern Italy, and the Crusades.
segments.body: The quoted account contrasts Viking infantry traditions with the Normans' reputation for mounted warfare and strategic fortification. It presents the eleventh century as an age of Norman expansion and connects that military culture to both conquest in Europe and the crusading movement.

evidence.note: The reading describes Norman heavy cavalry, fortification, territorial expansion, and involvement in the Crusades.

segments.title: Feudal armies and the military transition at Hastings
segments.summary: Montgomery's account uses Hastings to contrast balanced forces with feudal military organization and the prestige of the mounted knight.
segments.body: The passage argues that feudal society did not readily produce balanced, all-arms armies like those of the Eastern Roman Empire. It places the mounted knight at the center of medieval military prestige while noting that crusading armies mixed volunteers, peoples, and competing leaders in ways that made them less coherent.

evidence.note: The reading compares feudal forces, Eastern Roman combined arms, mounted knights, and mixed crusading armies.

segments.title: Channel patrols and mobilization before the Norman invasion
segments.summary: The 1066 campaign is framed as a prepared cross-Channel invasion opposed by seasonal patrols, limited service obligations, and professional housecarls.
segments.body: William's invasion appears as a long-prepared expansion rather than a sudden raid, while Harold must cover threats from both Normandy and Norway. English naval patrols watched the Channel through the summer, but the fyrd's limited service period constrained mobilization. Dr. Clarke adds context on the better-equipped professional housecarls who formed the household military core.

evidence.note: The transcript covers William's preparations, Harold's Channel patrols, fyrd service limits, and housecarls.

segments.title: Acre, Venice, and the maritime foundations of crusader power
segments.summary: Dan Jones's history shows how Acre's protected harbour, Venetian shipping, and colonial privileges sustained the Latin East.
segments.body: Acre is presented as a commercial and logistical hub whose artificial harbour works and defensive chain protected access from the sea. Venetian sailors received extensive privileges after helping conquer Tyre, binding the maritime republic's interests to the crusader states. The arrangement illustrates how rulers in Jerusalem depended on overseas shipping, military support, and self-governing merchant communities.

evidence.note: The reading describes Acre's harbour defences, Venetian naval assistance, colonial privileges, and the Templars.

segments.title: The Sicilian Vespers becomes a Mediterranean power struggle
segments.summary: A Mediterranean history traces how Sicily drew France, Aragon, Genoa, and the papacy into a conflict that distracted attention from the crusader states.
segments.body: The War of the Sicilian Vespers outgrows a local succession dispute as dynastic honor, papal prestige, Aragonese power, and Genoese alignment pull in much of the Mediterranean world. The reading contrasts these western preoccupations with the Mamluk focus on eliminating the remaining crusader states.

evidence.note: The passage links the Sicilian Vespers to French, Aragonese, Genoese, papal, and Mamluk strategic interests.

segments.title: Mongol diplomacy seeks a western alliance for the Holy Land
segments.summary: Mongol embassies propose coordinated action against the Mamluks, but sympathetic European courts offer no firm armies or timetable.
segments.body: Arghun's envoys travel among papal, French, and English courts seeking practical support for the threatened crusader states. A later proposal offers a Mongol army for a coordinated advance toward Damascus, yet western rulers still make no binding commitment. The episode shows how diplomatic reach did not translate into coalition warfare when European priorities had shifted.

evidence.note: The reading recounts Mongol embassies and an unsuccessful proposal for coordinated armies to recover the Holy Places.

segments.title: Tripoli, Acre, and the failure of maritime relief
segments.summary: The destruction of Tripoli prompts a Venetian-led galley response, but disorder at Acre helps end the truce before a much larger Mamluk army arrives.
segments.body: After the Mamluks take and destroy Tripoli, Venice sends twenty war galleys and Aragon adds five more to protect Christian interests at Acre. The accompanying adventurers attack Muslim residents, destroying the remaining truce and hardening the sultan's decision to eliminate the Frankish enclave. The account connects naval reinforcement, indiscipline ashore, and overwhelming enemy numbers in the final crisis of the crusader states.

evidence.note: The transcript covers Tripoli's fall, the Venetian and Aragonese galley force, violence at Acre, and the Mamluk advance.

segments.title: Henry V exploits France's political fracture
segments.summary: A lively Anglo-French history explains how civil conflict in France and Henry V's dynastic needs prepared the way for renewed invasion.
segments.body: The reading follows the deposition of Richard II, Charles VI's incapacity, and the violent rivalry between Burgundian and Armagnac factions. Henry V uses France's division to strengthen his territorial claims and his own position as a young king. His demands for marriage and the French crown become a pretext for a war whose troops and artillery were already being prepared.

evidence.note: The reading traces French factional conflict, Henry V's political needs, territorial bargaining, and preparations for war.

segments.title: Henry V's invasion fleet lands at Harfleur
segments.summary: The 1415 invasion uses a vast Solent fleet to carry soldiers, animals, artillery, and supplies to the Seine estuary at Harfleur.
segments.body: Henry chooses a Normandy landing rather than the familiar route through Calais and assembles a fleet described as roughly 1,500 ships. The vessels carry more than ten thousand fighters along with horses, cattle, and cannon, showing the scale of medieval amphibious logistics. Unloading at Harfleur takes two days before the army can begin its campaign.

evidence.note: The passage details the fleet's route, size, cargo, flagship, landing, and two-day unloading effort.

segments.title: Harfleur attrition and the road to Agincourt
segments.summary: The siege of Harfleur, disease losses, blocked crossings, and exhausted supplies turn Henry's invasion into the desperate march toward Agincourt.
segments.body: Harfleur's defenses hold long enough for disease and combat to kill or disable a large part of Henry's army, forcing him to abandon the advance on Paris. The retreat toward Calais is slowed by rain, destroyed crossings, and French blocking forces, adding a major detour while English supplies and morale collapse. The segment ends with the outnumbered army facing the French near Agincourt.

evidence.note: The reading covers Harfleur's siege, dysentery, English losses, blocked river crossings, and the approach to Agincourt.
